concept.headline: Every value has exactly one owner — when that owner leaves scope, the value is dropped.
concept.explanation: Rust achieves memory safety without a garbage collector through ownership. Three rules govern every value: 1) Each value has exactly one owner. 2) When the owner goes out of scope, the value is automatically freed (dropped). 3) There can only be one owner at a time. Assigning a heap-allocated value (like String) to another variable moves ownership — the original binding becomes invalid. Primitive types (i32, bool, etc.) implement the Copy trait and are duplicated instead.
keyPoints: Rule 1: Each value has exactly one owner at a time
Rule 2: When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (freed)
Rule 3: Assignment of heap types moves ownership — the old binding is invalid
Copy types (i32, f64, bool, char, tuples of Copy types) are duplicated, not moved
String is not Copy — it owns heap memory that cannot be cheaply duplicated

demo.intro: Watch how assigning a String moves ownership, while assigning an i32 copies it.

explanation.what: s1 owns a String on the heap
explanation.why: String::from allocates heap memory to store 'hello' and gives ownership of that allocation to s1
explanation.change: s1 = String pointing to heap data 'hello'
explanation.intent: Create a heap-allocated string — the owner is responsible for freeing it
explanation.misconception: String (capital S) is heap-allocated and owns its data. &str is a view into string data (a borrow). They are different types.

explanation.what: Ownership of the heap data moves from s1 to s2
explanation.why: Rust cannot have two owners for the same heap allocation — so s1 is invalidated and s2 becomes the sole owner
explanation.change: s2 owns heap data; s1 is now invalid (moved)
explanation.intent: Transfer the obligation to free the data from s1 to s2
explanation.misconception: This is NOT a copy of the heap data — Rust moves the pointer (stack metadata) and invalidates s1. No heap allocation happens.

explanation.why: s2 is the current owner of the heap string — it is valid to use
explanation.intent: Show that the moved value is accessible through the new owner
explanation.misconception: Using s1 here would be a compile error: 'use of moved value: s1'. Rust catches this statically.

explanation.why: i32 is stored on the stack and implements the Copy trait
explanation.intent: Demonstrate a Copy type to contrast with String's move behaviour
explanation.misconception: Stack-allocated types like i32 are trivially copyable — duplicating them is as cheap as duplicating a register value

explanation.what: n2 = n1 — value is COPIED, not moved
explanation.why: i32 implements Copy, so let n2 = n1 duplicates the bit pattern — n1 remains valid
explanation.change: n2 = 5 (new independent stack slot); n1 still valid
explanation.intent: Show that Copy types behave like you expect from most languages
explanation.misconception: This is not ownership transfer — both n1 and n2 independently own their own 5

explanation.what: Printing n1 and n2 — both valid
explanation.why: n1 was copied, not moved, so both bindings are usable
explanation.intent: Confirm that both n1 and n2 are independently valid after the copy
explanation.misconception: Had n1 been a String, using it here after let n2 = n1 would be a compile error

options: Line 3 — a was moved into b
prediction.explanation: let b = a moves ownership of the String from a to b. After the move, a is invalid. Printing a on Line 3 is a compile error: use of moved value. Line 4 is fine because b is the current owner.

explanation.afterDemo: String carries heap memory, so Rust enforces a single owner — assigning s1 to s2 moves the ownership pointer and invalidates s1. i32 sits on the stack and is trivially copied, so both n1 and n2 hold independent 5s. This is how Rust guarantees no double-free errors: at most one variable can own any heap allocation at a time.
keyTakeaways: Assigning a heap type (String) moves ownership — the old binding is invalidated.
Copy types (i32, bool, etc.) are duplicated — both old and new bindings stay valid.
When the owning variable goes out of scope, Rust automatically drops (frees) the heap memory.

practice.prompt: Create a String `s` with value "Rust". Move it into a function called `print_it` that takes a String and prints it. Then try to print `s` after the call — observe the compile error (in your head), then fix the code by cloning: pass s.clone() instead.
practice.hint: s.clone() creates a deep copy of the heap data — both the original and the copy are valid owners of separate allocations.

feedback.successMessage: Well done! clone() produces a fresh heap allocation, so both print_it and main get their own owned String.
retryHints: Passing s (without clone) to print_it moves s — the function owns it, and it is dropped when print_it returns.
Use s.clone() to create a deep copy and keep s valid in main.
Expected output: 'Rust' printed twice — once from print_it, once from main.

summary.title: Ownership — Complete
takeaways: Each value has exactly one owner; ownership moves on assignment of heap types.
When the owner's scope ends, the value is automatically freed.
Copy types are duplicated; heap types are moved. Use .clone() for an explicit deep copy.
